items.quote: There is now evidence that the brain functions differently for singing than it does for talking.
items.author: Barry Guitar
items.role: Ph.D., Professor of Communication Sciences and Disorders, University of Vermont

items.quote: In singing, we use our vocal chords, lips, and tongue differently than when we talk.
items.author: Barry Guitar
items.role: Ph.D., Professor of Communication Sciences and Disorders, University of Vermont

items.quote: The rhythmic pattern of music tends to help regulate a person's breathing.
items.author: Barry Guitar
items.role: Ph.D., Professor of Communication Sciences and Disorders, University of Vermont

items.quote: singing, reading, or speaking in unison may temporarily reduce stuttering
items.author: National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders
items.publication: NIDCD — What Is Stuttering? Diagnosis & Treatment

items.author: National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders
items.publication: NIDCD — What Is Stuttering? Diagnosis & Treatment

items.quote: Activity in the auditory cortex is heightened, however, under fluency-inducing conditions in which AWS temporarily become fluent while synchronizing their speech with an external rhythm, such as a metronome or another speaker.
items.author: Emily O. Garnett, Ho Ming Chow, Charles J. Limb, Yanni Liu, Soo-Eun Chang
items.role: Researchers in neuroscience and communication disorders

items.quote: The effects of singing on temporarily alleviating or improving stutters is well-documented.
items.author: Zoe McDonald
items.publication: Queensland Brain Institute, The University of Queensland

items.quote: singing engages the right hemisphere, and is therefore thought to play a compensatory role.
items.author: Zoe McDonald
items.publication: Queensland Brain Institute, The University of Queensland
